title: How the Eiffel Tower Resists Corrosion Through Design, Materials, and Maintenance

lesson.learn: Completed in 1889, the Eiffel Tower in Paris is constructed from over 18,000 pieces of puddled wrought iron and stands 330 meters tall. Despite being exposed to rain, wind, and urban pollution for more than 130 years, it has remained structurally sound due to a combination of material properties, structural design, and continuous maintenance. Rather than relying on modern anti-corrosion technologies, its longevity is ensured primarily through regular repainting cycles, careful drainage design, and the natural corrosion resistance of wrought iron when properly protected.
lesson.simpler: The Eiffel Tower lasts so long because it is made from strong iron, designed to shed water easily, and is repainted regularly to stop rust from forming. Think of it like a metal structure that is constantly given a protective coat to keep it safe from rain and air.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The Eiffel Tower’s durability comes from a combination of smart material choice, structural design that reduces moisture buildup, and a long-term maintenance strategy focused on regular protective painting.
lesson.deeperDive: A major factor in the Eiffel Tower’s resistance to corrosion is its use of puddled wrought iron, which contains fewer impurities than many other forms of iron used in the 19th century. Its open lattice structure allows wind and rain to pass through rather than accumulate moisture, reducing sustained corrosion risks. Most importantly, the tower is repainted approximately every 7 years using around 60 tons of paint, which acts as a continuous protective barrier against oxidation. Over time, different protective coatings and improved paint formulations have been used, but the core strategy remains consistent: prevent oxygen and moisture from reaching the iron surface for extended periods.
lesson.funFact: The Eiffel Tower is repainted by hand in sections, and workers must climb and carefully coat every surface. Over its lifetime, it has been painted in multiple shades of brown, with the current color known as “Eiffel Tower Brown.”

people: Gustave Eiffel - French engineer who designed and built the Eiffel Tower
Maurice Koechlin - Chief engineer who helped design the tower’s structural framework
Émile Nouguier - Engineer who contributed to the early structural concept of the tower
Stephen Sauvestre - Architect who refined the tower’s final aesthetic design
places: Paris, France - Location of the Eiffel Tower
Champ de Mars - Public park where the Eiffel Tower is situated
Seine River - Nearby river influencing local humidity and environmental exposure
years: 1887 - Construction of the Eiffel Tower begins
1889 - Eiffel Tower is completed for the Exposition Universelle
1930s - First major repainting cycles are standardized for long-term preservation
Every ~7 years - Regular maintenance repainting cycle continues into the present day
concepts: Wrought iron - A low-carbon iron material used in the Eiffel Tower’s construction
Corrosion protection - Preventing oxidation through coatings and maintenance
Protective painting - Repeated application of paint layers to block moisture and oxygen
Lattice structure - Open-frame design that reduces wind pressure and moisture retention
Material durability - Long-term performance of construction materials under environmental stress
